subject: Theranos, Elizabeth Holmes, and the line between a bold promise and criminal fraud
central_question: A single drop of blood, a nine-billion-dollar company, a celebrated young founder — and a machine that never actually worked. When does 'fake it till you make it' cross the line into a crime?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands how Theranos rose and collapsed, what a jury actually convicted Elizabeth Holmes of, and the legal line — intent to deceive — that separates a failed promise from criminal fraud.
surprise: The jury did not simply call Holmes a fraud across the board: it convicted her of defrauding investors, acquitted her on the patient counts, and deadlocked on others — a split that maps the exact, narrow line the law draws.
stakes: How the law decides when ambition and salesmanship become a crime — the question under every modern startup that 'sells the vision' before the product is real.
target_audience: English-speaking US general audience interested in business, fraud, tech and how the law works
demand_evidence: Theranos / Elizabeth Holmes is one of the most recognizable modern fraud stories; sustained, high-search, advertiser-safe true-business interest.
Recent (2022 conviction), self-relevant ('how a scam fooled everyone'), strong series finale.
competition: Heavily covered, but most retellings dramatize the personality; few center the neutral legal question — what makes it a crime, and what exactly was proven.
differentiation: Series FINALE that flips the lens: from the state's power over you (EP6-14) to one person's power over everyone. Neutral, record-based, centered on the legal line of intent.
research_feasibility: High: DOJ and SEC records, the public trial record, and major-outlet reporting are extensive and authoritative.
visual_feasibility: High: a single drop of blood; magazine-cover montage (symbolic, no real likeness); a courtroom; an animated 'count-by-count' verdict board; rise-and-fall valuation graph.
risk_notes: R3 — LIVING, CRIMINALLY CONVICTED person (Elizabeth Holmes); also Ramesh Balwani. Dedicated rights/legal review REQUIRED before publish.

Truth is the safeguard: state the FOUR investor-fraud CONVICTIONS as established fact (jury verdict; sentence imposed; conviction upheld on appeal). For the patient counts say 'acquitted / found not guilty'; for the deadlocked counts say 'the jury could not reach a verdict / mistrial' — NEVER 'guilty'.

Do NOT suggest the patient acquittals mean the technology worked or that patients were unharmed — acquittal means those specific counts were not proven beyond a reasonable doubt, not exoneration of the conduct. Balwani WAS convicted on patient counts.

Attribute conclusions about intent/knowledge to the jury/court, not as the narrator's independent assertion. No real-person likeness or deepfake of Holmes/Balwani (invariant 11); magazine-cover/visuals symbolic and disclosed.
Use precise figures: Holmes 135 months (~11 yrs 3 mos); Balwani ~155 months ('nearly 13 years'). Avoid implying the Walgreens rollout was nationwide (it was limited, mostly Arizona).
kill_conditions: Cannot source the convictions, acquittals, and sentences to DOJ/court records plus one independent reference; or cannot maintain strict record-based neutrality.
